skill.name: flux-kontext
skill.description: Edit images with Flux 1 Kontext Pro (Black Forest Labs' precise local image-edit model) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents Flux Kontext's strengths (single-reference precise local edits, strong prompt control, consistent high-fidelity outputs), the schema (single image + prompt), and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / GPT Image 2 edit / Flux 2 Klein instead. Calls `runcomfy run blackforestlabs/flux-1-kontext/pro/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "flux kontext", "flux-kontext", "flux 1 kontext", "kontext", "BFL kontext", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Precise local image editing with Flux 1 Kontext Pro on RunComfy, with optimized prompting patterns and routing guidance.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation-only skill that instructs the AI how to invoke the runcomfy CLI for Flux Kontext Pro image editing. The static scanner flagged 53 patterns, but all are false positives: backticks in Markdown code blocks (documentation, not execution), URLs in example commands (not actual network calls), and file paths in example invocations. The skill has no executable code, no obfuscation, and no prompt injection attempts. The declared behavior (CLI invocation with JSON payload) is transparent and well-documented in the Security & Privacy section.

medium_findings.title: CLI invocation documented in skill body
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the AI to invoke `runcomfy run blackforestlabs/flux-1-kontext/pro/edit` with user-supplied prompts and image URLs. While the CLI does not shell-expand inputs (documented at line 164), the AI is effectively a proxy that passes user content to an external service. This is the intended functionality, not a vulnerability, but it should be disclosed to end users.

low_findings.title: External image URL passed to remote model server
low_findings.description: The skill passes user-supplied image URLs to the RunComfy model server. The skill correctly notes (line 165) that image-based prompt injection is a known risk for image-edit models. This is a transparency note, not a vulnerability — the risk is inherent to the use case and is disclosed.

low_findings.title: Token stored in user config directory
low_findings.description: The RunComfy CLI stores the API token at `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with file mode 0600. This is a standard credential storage pattern. The skill documents this behavior and offers an env-var bypass for CI/container use cases.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Flux Kontext Pro on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Precise local image edits often fail because prompts are not shaped for the model. This skill bundles Flux 1 Kontext Pro's documented prompting patterns so edits come out sharp on the first try. It also routes you to the right sibling model when Kontext is not the best fit.
actual_capabilities: Invoke Flux 1 Kontext Pro via the runcomfy CLI with a single source image and a declarative edit prompt
Apply documented prompting patterns for precise local edits, identity preservation, and brand-asset text swaps
Support reproducible variant series via the seed parameter
Route to Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 edit, or Flux 2 Klein when the use case is multi-image, multilingual, or text-to-image
Handle CLI authentication via device-code login or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable
limitations: Supports a single source image only; multi-image workflows require Nano Banana Edit
Public RunComfy documentation is minimal; schema fields beyond prompt, image, aspect_ratio, and seed may exist and need verification on the model page
Compound prompts cause output drift and must be split into sequential single-instruction passes
Multilingual or embedded text editing is better handled by GPT Image 2 edit

use_cases.title: Product photo local edit
use_cases.description: Swap a brand name on a product label or change a small object in a marketing photo while preserving the rest of the image.

use_cases.title: Portrait micro-editing
use_cases.description: Add a prop, adjust expression, or change clothing color on a single portrait while keeping identity and pose intact.

use_cases.title: Design asset iteration
use_cases.description: Generate reproducible variants of a single design asset by fixing a seed and iterating on the edit prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Simple local edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this image: [describe the single change you want, in one declarative sentence].

prompt_templates.title: Preservation-led edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep [identity / pose / framing / brand] unchanged. Then [state the single change].
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate — when you need to protect parts of the source image

prompt_templates.title: Brand asset text swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the [bottle / packaging / layout] exactly as in the input. Replace only the [field name] text from "[old]" to "[new]". Same [font weight / color / position].
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate — targeted text or color replacement on branded assets

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step sequential edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Step 1: [change A]. Step 2: [change B]. Split into two separate runcomfy invocations to avoid drift.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced — compound changes that should be split into sequential passes

output_examples.input: User asks: "Make the person in this photo hold an orange umbrella."
output_examples.output: The skill constructs a runcomfy CLI invocation with a preservation-led prompt and the source image URL, runs it, and returns the edited image to the user via the --output-dir path.

output_examples.input: User asks: "Change the brand name on this bottle label from ALPHA to AURA."
output_examples.output: The skill uses a brand-asset prompt template, invokes the CLI with a seed for reproducibility, and downloads the result into the configured output directory.

best_practices: Lead prompts with a preservation clause before stating the change to protect identity and framing
Use one declarative instruction per invocation; split compound edits into sequential passes
Set an explicit seed when you need reproducible variant series
Authenticate via the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable in CI or container environments
anti_patterns: Do not combine multiple edits in a single prompt — Kontext drifts on compound instructions
Do not pass multiple source images — use Nano Banana Edit for multi-image flows
Do not use passive voice in prompts — imperative mood produces more reliable results

faq.question: What model does this skill use?
faq.answer: Flux 1 Kontext Pro from Black Forest Labs, hosted on the RunComfy Model API.

faq.question: Can I use multiple source images?
faq.answer: No. Kontext supports a single source image. For multi-image edits, use Nano Banana Edit instead.

faq.question: How do I authenticate in CI?
faq.answer: Set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable instead of running runcomfy login.

faq.question: Where is my API token stored?
faq.answer: After runcomfy login, the token is written to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600 (owner-only).

faq.question: What if my compound prompt drifts?
faq.answer: Split the edit into sequential single-instruction passes. Kontext is designed for one declarative change at a time.

faq.question: Is the prompt content shell-expanded?
faq.answer: No. The CLI transmits the JSON body directly to the Model API over HTTPS. There is no shell-injection surface from prompt content.
